# PromptForge: AI Toolkit — Native iOS Prompt Manager | $4.99 No Subscription
URL: https://chrisbusbin-pixel.github.io/promptforge-support/
Description: PromptForge is the native iOS prompt manager for ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, and other LLMs. Save, organize, and reuse prompts with variable templates. $4.99 one-time. No subscription. All data stays on your device.

PromptForge: AI Toolkit — Native iOS Prompt Manager | $4.99 No Subscription 

## PromptForge: AI Toolkit 
The native iOS prompt manager for ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, and any LLM. Save, organize, and reuse your best prompts. $4.99 one-time No subscription All data on-device 📱 Get on the App Store → FAQ Support 

## What PromptForge does 
Prompt Library Save and organize every prompt in one place. Folders with custom names and emoji icons. 
Variable Templater Use {{placeholders}} in your prompts for reusable templates that fill in on demand. Stop rewriting the same prompt with different inputs. 
Discover Tab 200+ curated prompt templates across 11 categories: writing, coding, marketing, education, image generation, business, productivity, learning, design, research, and more. 
Export / Import Full prompt library export to JSON. Move your data freely. Back up before reinstalling. Share collections. 
AI Subscription Tracker Built-in tool to track what you're spending each month on ChatGPT Plus, Claude Pro, Midjourney, Cursor, Perplexity Pro, etc. 
Privacy-First All data stored locally on-device using Apple's secure on-device storage. No accounts. No servers. No analytics. 
OTA Templates New prompt template packs delivered over-the-air without waiting for App Store updates. 

## How it compares 
PromptForge AIPRM PromptHero Notion AI 
Native iOS app ✅ ❌ Chrome only ❌ Web only ✅ 
One-time price ✅ $4.99 ❌ Subscription ❌ Marketplace fees ❌ Subscription 
Works offline ✅ ❌ ❌ Partial 
No account required ✅ ❌ ❌ ❌ 
Data stays on device ✅ ❌ ❌ ❌ 
Variable templates ✅ ❌ ❌ Partial 
AI sub tracker ✅ ❌ ❌ ❌ 

## Frequently Asked Questions 
What is PromptForge? A native iOS app for power users of ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, and other LLMs. Save your best prompts, organize them in folders, use {{variable}} templates, and track your AI subscription spend. 
How much does PromptForge cost? $4.99 USD as a one-time purchase. No subscription, no in-app purchases, no ads. 
Does PromptForge work offline? Yes. All prompts are stored locally on your device. No internet required. 
Is my data private? Absolutely. Zero data collection. All prompts and settings stored locally on-device. No servers, no accounts, no analytics. 
How is this different from AIPRM? AIPRM is a Chrome extension that requires an account and a subscription. PromptForge is a native iOS app, $4.99 one-time, no account, no subscription, and all data stays on your device. 
Will there be an Android or web version? Currently iOS-only. Email chrisbusbin@gmail.com if you'd like other platforms. 
Does it support image-gen prompts (Midjourney, Flux, etc.)? Yes. Prompts are AI-agnostic. Save Midjourney parameter prompts, Flux templates, ChatGPT prompts, Claude prompts — all in one library. 

## Get PromptForge 
📱 App Store — $4.99 

## Support 
See the support page or email chrisbusbin@gmail.com . We respond within 24 hours.

# AI Subscription Tracker for iPhone — Stop Paying for Tools You Don't Use
URL: https://chrisbusbin-pixel.github.io/promptforge-support/ai-subscription-tracker/
Description: The average AI power user pays for 3-7 different AI subscriptions and forgets at least one. Here's how to actually track and audit them — plus a built-in iOS tracker that lives inside PromptForge.

AI Subscription Tracker for iPhone — Stop Paying for Tools You Don't Use 
PromptForge › AI Subscription Tracker 

## AI Subscription Tracker for iPhone 
If you use AI tools heavily, you're probably paying for 3–7 different services and you've definitely forgotten at least one. Here's how to actually audit them — and the built-in tracker inside PromptForge. 📱 Get PromptForge — $4.99 About PromptForge 

## The forgotten-subscription problem 
Apple's built-in subscription manager (Settings → Apple ID → Subscriptions) only tracks things you bought through the App Store. AI tools mostly aren't sold through the App Store — they're billed directly by OpenAI, Anthropic, Midjourney, etc. via credit card. 
Result: most AI users have no central view of what they're paying for AI tools. The credit card statement just shows charges from "OPENAI" or "ANTHROPIC" with no context. 
The forgotten subscription is the most expensive one. The Midjourney plan you signed up for in March, used for two weeks, never canceled — that's $30/month for 9 months = $270 wasted before you noticed. 

## The typical AI subscription stack 
Common stacks for AI power users (estimated monthly spend, US pricing as of writing — confirm current prices on each company's site): 
Tool Tier Monthly cost 
ChatGPT Plus Standard ~$20 
Claude Pro Standard ~$20 
Midjourney Basic ~$10 
Midjourney Pro ~$30 
Perplexity Pro Standard ~$20 
Cursor Pro ~$20 
GitHub Copilot Individual ~$10 
ElevenLabs Starter / Creator ~$5–22 
Descript Creator / Pro ~$12–24 
Runway Standard ~$15 
Notion AI Add-on ~$10 
Typical "creator" stack ~$80–$120/month 
Typical "developer" stack ~$40–$70/month 
That's $480–$1,440/year. Worth tracking. 
The audit math: if your subscription audit reveals one $20/month tool you don't actually use, canceling it saves $240/year. Multiply by 2–3 tools and you're saving $500–$700/year. Almost no other 30-minute task generates that ROI. 

## The 3 ways to track AI subscriptions 

## 1. A spreadsheet (free, manual) 
Columns: Tool name, plan, monthly cost, billing date, last used date. Update monthly. Works fine if you actually maintain it. Most people don't. 

## 2. A general subscription tracker app (Bobby, Subby, Subscriptions) 
Tracks all recurring charges, not just AI. Often subscription-based itself (irony). Good if you want to see total household subscription spend. 

## 3. PromptForge's built-in AI subscription tracker 
Specifically tuned for AI tools. Lives inside the app you're already using to manage prompts. No additional app to install. No additional subscription. On-device. 

## What PromptForge's tracker does 
List your AI tools — name, monthly cost, billing date, plan tier 
Auto-calculated total monthly + annual spend 
Mark each subscription as "active" or "paused" 
Flag tools you haven't used recently (you set the threshold) 
Upcoming renewal reminders 
Annual cost view (helps with the "is this worth it" decision) 
All on-device — no bank login, no credit card data, no third-party servers 

## The 4-step audit (do this once a quarter) 

## Step 1: List every AI tool you've signed up for 
Check email for "Welcome to..." messages from AI tools over the past 12 months. Check credit card statement for AI-related charges (search for company names). The list is usually longer than you remember. 

## Step 2: For each tool, answer one question 
"Did I use this in the past 30 days?" Yes = keep. No = strong cancel candidate. Maybe = downgrade to free tier and see if you actually miss it. 

## Step 3: Cancel the dead ones immediately 
Don't wait. Don't think "I might use it next month." If you didn't use it last month, you won't use it next month. Cancel. 

## Step 4: Re-audit in 90 days 
Most people add 1–2 new AI subscriptions per quarter. Without quarterly audits, the stack creeps up to $200/month. 

## The "is it worth it" tests 
Per tool, ask: 
Frequency: Use 3+ times/week? Worth it. Once a week or less? Free tier. 
Replaceability: Could I get the same value from a free tool with slightly worse UX? Often yes — most paid AI tools have free alternatives. 
Stack overlap: Am I paying ChatGPT Plus AND Claude Pro? One usually suffices for most people. 
Annual math: $20/month is $240/year. Would I write a $240 check today for this? Often a no. 

## FAQ 
Is PromptForge an actual subscription tracker, or just a prompt manager? It's both. The primary use is prompt management; the subscription tracker is a built-in module. Many users report the tracker alone has saved them more than the $4.99 app price within the first month. 
Does PromptForge access my bank or credit card? No. You manually enter what you're paying for. By design — keeps your financial credentials private. 
Can I track non-AI subscriptions too? Yes. The tracker doesn't enforce "AI only" — you can add Netflix, gym, anything recurring. AI is just the most common use case. 
What if pricing changes? Update the cost in PromptForge whenever you get a notification of a price change. The app stores what you tell it. 
Does ChatGPT Plus auto-renew? Yes — most AI subscriptions auto-renew monthly. That's why tracking matters; without a tracker, the renewal happens silently and the unused subscription continues for months. 

## Get PromptForge 
📱 App Store — $4.99 one-time 
© 2026 Chris Busbin. AI tool prices are estimates as of writing — verify current pricing on each company's website. Back to PromptForge · ChatGPT prompt manager · Midjourney prompt manager

# AIPRM Alternative for iPhone — PromptForge ($4.99 One-Time, No Subscription)
URL: https://chrisbusbin-pixel.github.io/promptforge-support/aiprm-alternative/
Description: Looking for an AIPRM alternative on iOS? PromptForge is the native iPhone prompt manager: $4.99 one-time, no subscription, no account, all prompts on-device. Save and reuse ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini prompts.

AIPRM Alternative for iPhone — PromptForge ($4.99 One-Time, No Subscription) 
PromptForge › AIPRM Alternative 

## AIPRM Alternative for iPhone 
PromptForge is the native iOS app you've been looking for. $4.99 one-time. No subscription. No account. All your prompts stay on your device. 📱 Get PromptForge — $4.99 More about PromptForge 

## The short answer 
If you've been using AIPRM in Chrome on desktop and want something that works the same way on iPhone, you're in the right place. AIPRM doesn't have a native iOS app — it's a browser extension. PromptForge is a native iPhone and iPad app that does what AIPRM does and more, without the subscription, without the account requirement, and without sending your prompts to a server. 

## How PromptForge compares to AIPRM 
PromptForge AIPRM 
Native iOS / iPad app ✅ Yes ❌ Chrome extension only 
Pricing model $4.99 one-time Free tier + subscriptions 
Account required ❌ No ✅ Yes 
Data stored on-device ✅ Yes ❌ Server-side 
Works offline ✅ Yes ❌ No 
Variable templater {{name}} ✅ Yes Limited 
Image-gen prompts (Midjourney, Flux) ✅ Yes Partial 
Built-in AI subscription tracker ✅ Yes ❌ No 
Curated prompt library ✅ 200+ templates, 11 categories ✅ Large library, account-gated 
Discover Tab / template feed ✅ OTA updates ✅ Account required 

## Why people switch from AIPRM to PromptForge 
1. They want something that works on their phone. AIPRM is desktop-Chrome-first. Most people read, save, and reuse AI prompts on their phone — between meetings, in line for coffee, anywhere. A Chrome extension can't help you there. 
2. They don't want a subscription for prompt management. Saving text snippets shouldn't cost a recurring fee. PromptForge is $4.99 once. That's it. 
3. They don't want to create yet another account. AIPRM requires sign-up. PromptForge requires zero — open the app and start saving. 
4. They want their prompts private. AIPRM stores prompts on its servers. PromptForge stores everything locally on your device using Apple's secure on-device storage. No servers, no analytics, no tracking. If you delete the app, your data is gone — only on your device. 

## What PromptForge does that AIPRM doesn't 
Variable templater. Use {{topic}} , {{audience}} , {{tone}} placeholders in any saved prompt. Tap the prompt and PromptForge pops up a fill-in form. Type the values, tap Run, and the final prompt copies to your clipboard ready to paste into ChatGPT, Claude, or any LLM. 
AI subscription tracker. Track what you're spending each month on ChatGPT Plus, Claude Pro, Midjourney, Cursor, Perplexity Pro. See your total LLM spend at a glance. 
Image-gen support. Save Midjourney parameter strings, Flux templates, Stable Diffusion negative prompts — all in the same library as your text prompts. 
Native iOS feel. Haptic feedback, gesture support, Spotlight integration, Share Sheet support. It's a real iPhone app, not a port. 

## FAQ 
Does AIPRM work on iPhone? AIPRM is a Chrome extension and doesn't have a native iOS app. There are workarounds with mobile browsers that support extensions, but the experience is poor on a phone. For a true native iPhone alternative, PromptForge is the answer. 
Can I import my AIPRM prompts? Yes — PromptForge supports JSON import. Export your prompts from any source and import via Settings → Import Prompts. 
Does PromptForge work with ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini? PromptForge is AI-agnostic. Save your prompts once and use them across any LLM. Tap a prompt, the final filled-in version copies to your clipboard, paste it into the AI app of your choice. 
What about Android or web? Currently iOS only (iPhone + iPad). Email chrisbusbin@gmail.com if you need other platforms. 
Is my data private? Yes. PromptForge stores all data locally on your device. Zero data collection, no analytics, no servers, no accounts. 

## Get PromptForge 
📱 App Store — $4.99 one-time

# Best AI Tools for Small Business (2026) — Honest Stack for Solopreneurs
URL: https://chrisbusbin-pixel.github.io/promptforge-support/best-ai-tools-for-small-business/
Description: An honest stack of AI tools for small business owners and solopreneurs. What to actually pay for, what works free, and where to focus your AI budget if you have less than $100/month for tools.

Best AI Tools for Small Business (2026) — Honest Stack for Solopreneurs 
PromptForge › AI Tools for Small Business 

## Best AI Tools for Small Business (2026) 
An honest stack for solopreneurs and small teams. What to actually pay for. What works free. Where to focus if you have under $100/month for AI tools. 📱 Get PromptForge — $4.99 About PromptForge 
Disclaimer: AI tool pricing changes monthly. Confirm current prices on each company's site. PromptForge is one of the apps reviewed but isn't the central tool — it's the prompt library that sits alongside whichever main AI you choose. 

## The honest hierarchy 
For a small business with limited budget, here's the priority order for AI spending: 
Pick one general-purpose AI (Claude or ChatGPT) — covers 60% of use cases 
Add a prompt manager ($4.99 one-time PromptForge or similar) — turns the AI into a productivity machine 
Add ONE specialized tool for your highest-volume function (image gen, customer service, bookkeeping, etc.) 
Resist adding more tools until you're consistently hitting limits on the first three 

## By function — what to use 

## Writing + customer comms 
Task Best tool Free option 
Email drafting Claude or ChatGPT Free tier of either 
Customer service responses Claude (long context, careful tone) Free tier 
Marketing copy Claude for long-form, ChatGPT for headlines Free tiers 
Sales outreach Claude or ChatGPT with prompt templates Free tiers + PromptForge 
SEO content Claude for drafting, Surfer/Frase for keyword research Claude free tier 

## Visual content 
Task Best tool Free option 
Marketing images Midjourney or Flux Limited free credits via various platforms 
Social media graphics Canva (with Magic Studio AI) Canva free tier 
Product photography Midjourney + Photoshop AI — 
Video Runway, Sora (limited access) — 
Voiceover ElevenLabs Limited free monthly minutes 

## Operations + admin 
Task Best tool Free option 
Meeting notes Otter, Fireflies, Granola Limited free minutes 
Email organization Superhuman AI, Shortwave Native Gmail labels + filters 
Document analysis Claude (large context) Free tier 
Research Perplexity Pro, Claude Perplexity free, Claude free 
Scheduling Calendly + Reclaim Free tier of either 

## Customer service 
Task Best tool Notes 
Chatbot for website Intercom Fin, Zendesk AI Pricey — only worth it at meaningful support volume 
Email support drafting Claude with company-specific prompts Use a prompt library to maintain consistent voice 
Knowledge base HelpScout, Zendesk, Notion AI search on top of a real knowledge base 

## Bookkeeping + finance 
Task Best tool Notes 
Bookkeeping QuickBooks, Xero, FreshBooks Don't try to "AI hack" bookkeeping — use real accounting software 
Receipt scanning Expensify, Dext OCR + AI categorization 
Tax research Claude or Perplexity Always verify with a CPA before acting 
Financial analysis Claude on exported data Don't share live bank data 

## The minimum viable AI stack ($25–$30/month) 
For a solopreneur on a tight budget: 
Claude Pro or ChatGPT Plus (~$20/mo) — pick one, not both 
PromptForge ($4.99 one-time) — the prompt library that makes the AI 10× more efficient 
Free Canva for social graphics 
Free Otter / Fathom for meeting notes That's $25/month + $5 one-time. Covers ~80% of small-business AI use cases. 

## The "I have $100/month for AI" stack 
For a small business with more breathing room: 
Claude Pro AND ChatGPT Plus (~$40/mo) — different models for different tasks 
Midjourney Basic (~$10/mo) — branded marketing imagery 
Canva Pro (~$15/mo) — design with AI tools and templates 
Perplexity Pro (~$20/mo) — research with cited sources 
PromptForge ($4.99 one-time) — prompt library 
Otter or Fathom Pro (~$10/mo) — meeting transcripts ~$95/month. Covers nearly every small-business AI use case professionally. 

## What to be cautious about 

## "Done-for-you" AI marketing platforms 
$300–$800/month tools that bundle prompts on top of GPT-4 or Claude. Most of what they do can be replicated with Claude + a good prompt library at a fraction of the cost. Buy these only if you genuinely don't have time to maintain your own prompt library. 

## AI sales/outreach automation 
Many AI tools promise to "personalize" outreach at scale. The reality: customers can usually tell. Generic AI personalization often performs worse than honest non-personalized outreach. Use AI to draft , then have humans personalize the top 20%. 

## Generic chatbots without customization 
An off-the-shelf chatbot that doesn't know your products, policies, or customers will give wrong answers and frustrate users. If you can't invest in customizing it, you're better off without it. 

## Tools that lock you into proprietary formats 
Some AI tools store your work in formats you can't easily export. Before adopting, verify export options. Lock-in is the hidden cost. 

## The prompt library multiplier 
The single biggest force multiplier for small-business AI use isn't a fancier tool — it's having your reusable prompts organized: 
Cold email templates (different industries, different roles) 
Customer service response templates (refunds, complaints, inquiries) 
Social media post structures (per platform) 
Sales call prep frameworks 
Vendor negotiation scripts 
Hiring + interview question banks 
Standard operating procedures (SOPs) 
Product description structures 
That's PromptForge's job — store, organize, and one-tap deploy these prompts across whichever AI you use. 

## What NOT to use AI for in a small business 
Legal contracts — use a real lawyer, or a vetted template service like LegalZoom 
Tax filing — use TurboTax, a CPA, or accounting software with built-in AI 
Medical / health-related claims — liability + accuracy issues 
Final hiring decisions — bias risk; use AI for screening only with human review 
Sensitive HR situations — performance reviews, terminations require human judgment 
Anything that requires verifiable facts without independent verification 

## FAQ 
Should I use ChatGPT or Claude for my business? Try both free tiers for two weeks. Pick the one whose voice and answers you prefer for your specific tasks. They're roughly comparable for most business uses. 
Are AI tools tax-deductible? Generally yes for business use — same as any other business software subscription. Track them. Talk to your accountant. 
Can AI replace my marketing person? For solopreneurs without a marketing person, AI helps you do marketing yourself. For businesses with a marketing person, AI makes them 2–3× more productive — replacing them usually backfires. 
What about data privacy? For sensitive business data (customer info, financials), use the AI's "no training on my data" tier (typically a paid tier on Claude/ChatGPT) or use enterprise versions. Free tiers may use your inputs for training. 
Should I learn to code with AI? If you have any technical inclination, AI coding tools (Cursor, Copilot) make basic automation accessible. Worth experimenting with for "small scripts that automate annoying repetitive tasks." 

## Get PromptForge 
📱 App Store — $4.99 one-time 
© 2026 Chris Busbin. AI tool capabilities and pricing change frequently — verify current details on each tool's website. Back to PromptForge · AI tools for writers · AI subscription tracker · ChatGPT prompt manager

# Best AI Tools for Students (2026) — Honest Guide for High School + College
URL: https://chrisbusbin-pixel.github.io/promptforge-support/best-ai-tools-for-students/
Description: An honest guide to AI tools for students. What helps you actually learn, what just helps you get the assignment done, and how to use AI without crossing your school's academic integrity line.

Best AI Tools for Students (2026) — Honest Guide for High School + College 
PromptForge › AI Tools for Students 

## Best AI Tools for Students (2026) 
An honest guide. Some AI tools genuinely help you learn. Others just help you skip the learning. Here's how to tell the difference, plus the academic integrity rules to know. 📱 Get PromptForge — $4.99 About PromptForge 
The honest framing: AI tools that explain things to you help you learn. AI tools that do the assignment for you may help you finish it but cost you the learning. Both have a place, but be honest with yourself about which you're using. 

## By academic function — what to use 

## Understanding course material 
NotebookLM (Google) — free with Google account. Upload your textbook chapters, lecture notes, professor's slides. Ask questions, get cited answers grounded in YOUR materials. Plus the audio overview feature creates a podcast-style summary you can listen to while commuting. 

Claude or ChatGPT — for "explain this concept differently" or "explain X like I'm 12." When the textbook isn't clicking, asking AI to re-explain in different ways often works. 

## Math + science tutoring 
Khanmigo (Khan Academy) — designed for guided learning, not answer-giving. Walks you through problems instead of solving them. Generally aligns with academic integrity policies. 

Wolfram Alpha — for math computation + step-by-step solutions. Has been a student tool since long before "AI" was the term. 

Photomath — snap a math problem; get step-by-step solution. Great for checking your work; problematic if used to skip learning. 

## Writing + essays 
Claude or ChatGPT for brainstorming — "what are 10 angles I could take on this topic?" Use these for outline + structure, write the essay yourself. 

Claude or ChatGPT for editing — paste your draft, ask for feedback on clarity, structure, argument strength. Then revise. The revision is the learning. 

Grammarly — grammar + style suggestions. Generally allowed by most schools. 

What NOT to do — generate the essay body and submit it. Most schools' AI detection is unreliable, but you'll get caught eventually, and you skipped the learning either way. 

## Research + sources 
Perplexity — search-grounded answers with citations. Great starting point for finding sources to read fully. 

NotebookLM — when you have a corpus of papers/sources, working through them. 

Google Scholar — still the gold standard for finding peer-reviewed sources. 

Caution: always verify AI-cited sources actually exist and say what AI claimed. Hallucinated citations are a known problem. Click through to the actual source. 

## Note-taking + lecture summaries 
Otter.ai — records lectures, transcribes them, summarizes. Free tier covers light use. 

Notion AI — if you take notes in Notion, the built-in AI summarizes, turns into flashcards, generates outlines. 

Always check your professor's recording policy — recording lectures without permission is often against course policies. 

## Studying + flashcards 
Quizlet (with AI features) — AI-generated flashcards from notes. Spaced repetition built in. 

Anki — the spaced-repetition standard. Free, open-source, learning-curve heavier than Quizlet. 

Generate flashcards from AI — paste your study guide into Claude or ChatGPT, ask for flashcard questions and answers. Import into Anki or Quizlet. 

## Coding (if applicable) 
GitHub Copilot — free for students with GitHub Student Pack. AI code assistant in your editor. 

Cursor — AI-first code editor. Strong free tier. 

Claude or ChatGPT — for explaining code, debugging help, learning new languages. 

Reality check: if you skip writing the code yourself in CS101, you'll struggle in CS201 when concepts are assumed. Use AI to explain, not to skip. 

## Language learning 
Duolingo Max — AI-powered conversation practice in target language. Premium tier ($). 

ChatGPT or Claude — practice conversations in target language. Ask for corrections + explanations. 

## The academic integrity reality 
Most universities have updated AI policies between 2023–2025. Common rules: 
Allowed (usually): AI for brainstorming, outlining, getting feedback on drafts, explaining concepts, generating practice problems 
Not allowed (usually): AI generating the body of submitted work, AI doing the work for you, undisclosed AI use 
Disclose if asked: Many schools now require disclosure of AI use in some assignments 
Read YOUR specific course's syllabus. Read YOUR specific institution's AI policy. They vary widely. Ask your professor when unclear — they'd rather answer the question than catch you violating the policy. 

## The "which is cheating" framework 
Use Generally OK Generally not OK 
Concept explanation "Explain photosynthesis" — 
Practice problems "Generate 10 calculus problems on derivatives" — 
Brainstorming "What are 10 essay topic angles?" — 
Editing feedback "Critique my draft" → you revise — 
Summarizing your notes "Summarize my chapter notes" — 
Generating essay body — "Write a 5-page essay on X" 
Solving graded problems — "Solve problem 3 from my homework" 
Writing code for a graded assignment — "Write the function I'm supposed to write" 
Generating a quiz answer — "What's the answer to this quiz question?" 

## The student AI stack ($0–$15/month) 
NotebookLM — free, course-material assistant 
Claude or ChatGPT free tier — explanations, brainstorming 
Khan Academy / Khanmigo — free, especially for math/science 
Anki — free, spaced-repetition flashcards 
PromptForge — $4.99 one-time, save your study prompts (study guides, essay outlines, exam prep frameworks) 
GitHub Student Pack — free for verified students; includes GitHub Copilot 
Optional: Otter.ai free tier — lecture transcription 
That covers a typical student's full AI needs. 

## How PromptForge fits a student workflow 
You'll write similar prompts repeatedly: 
"Explain X concept to me as if I'm a high school student" 
"Generate 10 practice problems on Y topic" 
"Critique my essay structure" 
"Summarize the key claims in this paper" 
"What are the counterarguments to X position?" 
"Generate flashcard Q&A from this study guide" 
Save these as templates in PromptForge. Reuse all semester. Stop rewriting the same prompts. 

## Ethics + practical advice 

## Be honest with yourself about why you're using AI 
"I want to understand this concept faster" is great. "I don't want to do this assignment" is a red flag — both ethically and because you'll struggle when the next assignment assumes you learned this one. 

## The AI detection arms race is unwinnable 
AI detection tools have high false-positive rates (especially on edited AI text or text by non-native English speakers). They're unreliable from both sides. Don't rely on AI detection to "prove" you didn't use AI; don't rely on tricks to evade detection. Just stay within your school's policy. 

## The skill you're losing is your future advantage 
If you skip writing essays in college, you don't develop writing skills. If you skip solving problems in CS, you don't develop problem-solving skills. AI in 2026 is good at producing decent average work — your competitive edge in the job market will increasingly be the skills you developed BY doing the work yourself. 

## FAQ 
What's the best free AI for students? For most: NotebookLM (Google account), free Claude tier, free ChatGPT tier, Khan Academy / Khanmigo. Combined, these cover most student use cases at $0/month. 
Will my professor know if I use AI? AI detection tools are unreliable. Professors often spot AI-generated work by tone, style inconsistencies, generic content, or because the writing doesn't match your previous work. Don't risk it for graded work. 
Should I tell my professor I used AI? Read the assignment. If disclosure is required, disclose. If not specified, ask. Most professors prefer transparency. 
Are there student discounts on paid AI tools? YNAB, Notion, GitHub (Copilot), Adobe, JetBrains all have student programs. ChatGPT and Claude don't currently have specific student discounts but free tiers are robust. 
Can AI replace my professor? No. Professors provide context, feedback, mentorship, accountability, and explanation tuned to YOUR specific confusion. AI is a study aid; professors are teachers. 

## Get PromptForge 
📱 App Store — $4.99 one-time 
© 2026 Chris Busbin. Back to PromptForge · AI for writers · AI for small business · ChatGPT prompt manager

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


# Best AI Tools for Writers (2026) — Honest Comparison: ChatGPT, Claude, Sudowrite, NovelCrafter
URL: https://chrisbusbin-pixel.github.io/promptforge-support/best-ai-tools-for-writers/
Description: An honest comparison of AI tools for writers. ChatGPT vs Claude vs Sudowrite vs NovelCrafter — what each is actually good at, what's overhyped, and how to build a working writing stack.

Best AI Tools for Writers (2026) — Honest Comparison: ChatGPT, Claude, Sudowrite, NovelCrafter 
PromptForge › Best AI Tools for Writers 

## Best AI Tools for Writers (2026) 
An honest comparison. Different writers need different tools — fiction vs non-fiction vs marketing vs journalism are genuinely different workflows. Here's what each tool is actually good at, and how to build a stack. 📱 Get PromptForge — $4.99 About PromptForge 
Disclaimer: Tool capabilities and pricing change frequently in the AI space. Verify current details on each tool's website. PromptForge (one of the apps reviewed) is included in the recommended stack but isn't pretending to be a writing tool itself — it's a prompt manager that works alongside whichever writing AI you choose. 

## The honest categorization 

## If you write FICTION (novels, short stories, novellas) 
Best dedicated tools: Sudowrite , NovelCrafter , Novelist.ai . These bundle chapter management, character cards, world-building, and AI generation into one app. Subscription pricing. 
Best general-purpose: Claude (longer context, tighter style adherence). ChatGPT for brainstorming and quick scene generation. 

## If you write LONG-FORM NON-FICTION (books, essays, longform articles) 
Best general-purpose: Claude (200K+ context window). Strongly preferred for substantial editing passes on long documents. 
Honorable mention: ChatGPT with the right prompt structure . 

## If you write CONTENT MARKETING / SEO BLOG POSTS 
Best general-purpose: Claude for the writing, ChatGPT for outlines. 
SEO-specific tools: Jasper , Copy.ai , Surfer SEO with AI . These are marketing-prompt-templates on top of GPT-4/Claude. Useful if you don't want to maintain your own prompt library. 

## If you write JOURNALISM / RESEARCH 
Best for research synthesis: Perplexity Pro (cited sources, search-grounded). Claude for source-rich documents (large context). NotebookLM for working with a fixed source corpus. 
What to avoid: any AI tool for fact-generation. Always use AI for synthesis and structure, then verify facts independently. 

## If you write SCREENPLAYS 
Specialized: WriterDuet , Final Draft (with AI assistant features). General-purpose Claude or ChatGPT for scene drafting. 

## The contenders, individually 

## ChatGPT (OpenAI) 
Best for: Quick generation, brainstorming, breaking writers' block, iterative editing. 
Strengths: Fast. Strong at following structured instructions. Excellent at brainstorming long lists of options. Custom GPTs let you bundle prompts. 
Weaknesses: Tendency toward generic, hedging prose. Shorter context than Claude. Voice consistency over long pieces requires explicit prompting. 
Pricing: Free tier substantial; Plus subscription for more (~$20/mo as of writing). 

## Claude (Anthropic) 
Best for: Long-form work. Long-document editing. Style-consistent generation. Source-grounded synthesis. 
Strengths: Very long context window. Better at following style guidelines. Less tendency to hedge. Generally produces tighter prose for serious writing. 
Weaknesses: Slightly slower than GPT-4o for short tasks. Some users find the writing voice "polite" or restrained — adjust with explicit voice instructions. 
Pricing: Free tier substantial; Pro subscription for more (~$20/mo as of writing). 

## Sudowrite 
Best for: Fiction writers (especially novelists). 
Strengths: Designed for fiction workflow. Brainstorm / Describe / Rewrite / Expand commands cover most fiction generation needs without writing prompts. 
Weaknesses: Subscription pricing is meaningful. Not useful for non-fiction or marketing. Some writers find the "voice" of generations less customizable. 

## NovelCrafter 
Best for: Long-form fiction with extensive world-building. 
Strengths: Strong codex/wiki feature for tracking characters, locations, lore. Connects to multiple AI providers (you bring your own API key). 
Weaknesses: Steeper learning curve than Sudowrite. BYO-API model means you're also managing API costs separately. 

## Perplexity Pro 
Best for: Research-heavy writing, journalism, fact-driven non-fiction. 
Strengths: Cited sources for everything. Search-grounded responses reduce hallucination. 
Weaknesses: Generation quality not as polished as ChatGPT/Claude. Better for research-into-bullets than research-into-final-prose. 

## Jasper / Copy.ai 
Best for: Marketing teams who don't want to maintain their own prompt library. 
Strengths: Pre-built marketing templates (cold emails, landing pages, headlines). Brand-voice features. 
Weaknesses: Substantially more expensive than base Claude/ChatGPT. The underlying AI is the same models; you're paying for templates. 

## Grammarly + ProWritingAid 
Best for: Editing and proofreading. 
Strengths: Catches grammar, style, and clarity issues that AI generation tools don't. 
Weaknesses: Different category — these are editing tools, not generation tools. Use alongside, not instead of, generation AI. 

## NotebookLM (Google) 
Best for: Working with a fixed corpus of sources (research papers, source documents, transcripts). 
Strengths: Grounded in your uploaded sources only. Audio overview feature is genuinely useful for absorbing research. 
Weaknesses: Not a generator — better used for research synthesis than draft creation. 

## PromptForge (the app you're on now) 
Best for: Storing and reusing the prompts that work — across whatever AI tool you use. 
Strengths: $4.99 one-time, no subscription. Variable templates with {{placeholder}} support. Works with any text-based AI tool. On-device. 
What it isn't: A writing app. PromptForge stores and copies prompts; you generate in ChatGPT/Claude/etc. 

## Quick comparison (as of writing) 
Tool Best for Pricing model 
ChatGPT Plus Brainstorm + quick gen Subscription 
Claude Pro Long-form + editing Subscription 
Sudowrite Fiction Subscription 
NovelCrafter Fiction + world-building Subscription + API 
Perplexity Pro Research synthesis Subscription 
Jasper / Copy.ai Marketing templates Subscription (higher tier) 
NotebookLM Source-grounded work Free (Google account) 
Grammarly Editing Free + premium 
PromptForge Prompt storage across all of the above $4.99 one-time 

## The recommended stack by writer type 

## Fiction novelist 
Sudowrite OR NovelCrafter for project + generation. Claude for long editing passes. PromptForge to save the brainstorming prompts that worked. Grammarly for line edits. 

## Content marketer / SEO writer 
Claude for drafting (longer context, better style consistency). ChatGPT for quick brainstorming. PromptForge for your library of headline / outline / CTA / meta description templates. Surfer SEO or similar for keyword research. 

## Journalist / longform non-fiction 
Perplexity Pro for research with sources. Claude for synthesis and drafting. PromptForge to save your interview question templates and editing prompts. 

## Newsletter / personal essay writer 
Claude or ChatGPT for the writing itself. PromptForge for saved prompts (newsletter formats, opening hook variations, CTA structures). Grammarly for final polish. 

## The honest case against AI for writers 
Some writing benefits from struggle. Some pieces are better when you wrote them yourself, slowly, fighting through the wrong sentences. AI tools can shortcut the part that was actually doing the creative work. 
Use AI for: 
Brainstorming (10× faster) 
Editing and consistency checks 
Headline / title brainstorming 
Research synthesis 
Outlines and structure 
Breaking writers' block (a draft to react to) 
Be cautious about using AI for: 
Final prose in personal essays (your voice matters) 
Anything where you're being paid for your distinct perspective 
Source-of-truth fact generation (always verify) 
Writing meant to be intimate, vulnerable, or specifically yours 

## FAQ 
What's the cheapest AI writing setup? Free ChatGPT + free Claude + PromptForge ($4.99 one-time). Covers ~80% of what most writers need. Upgrade to Plus/Pro tiers only when you hit free-tier limits regularly. 
Is AI-written content penalized by Google? Google's stated position is that AI-assisted content is fine if it's high-quality and provides original value. Pure AI-generated thin content tends to underperform. Use AI as an assistant, not a replacement. 
Can I use AI for my novel? Many published novelists do. The rules around AI disclosure vary by publisher and contest. Check before submitting. 
What about AI detection tools? AI detection is unreliable. False positives are common, especially on edited AI text or text written by non-native speakers. Don't rely on these tools as ground truth either way. 

## Get PromptForge 
📱 App Store — $4.99 one-time 
© 2026 Chris Busbin. AI tool capabilities and pricing change frequently — verify current details on each tool's website. Back to PromptForge · ChatGPT prompt manager · Organize prompts

# The Best ChatGPT Prompt Manager for iPhone (2026) — PromptForge
URL: https://chrisbusbin-pixel.github.io/promptforge-support/chatgpt-prompt-manager/
Description: Most ChatGPT users save prompts in Notes, screenshots, or random docs. A real prompt manager organizes them in folders, runs variable templates, and works offline. Here's how to pick one — and how PromptForge compares.

The Best ChatGPT Prompt Manager for iPhone (2026) — PromptForge 
PromptForge › ChatGPT Prompt Manager 

## The Best ChatGPT Prompt Manager for iPhone (2026) 
If your "prompt library" is currently a mix of Apple Notes, screenshots, and a few starred ChatGPT chats — you're losing prompts, copy-pasting friction, and rebuilding the same instructions over and over. A purpose-built prompt manager fixes that. 📱 Get PromptForge — $4.99 About PromptForge 

## What "prompt manager" actually means 
A prompt manager is a dedicated app for storing, organizing, and reusing the AI prompts you write. Instead of treating prompts as throwaway text, you treat them like recipes — written once, refined over time, used hundreds of times. 
Good prompt managers have: 
Folders or tags by use case, AI tool, or project 
Variable templates with {{placeholder}} fill-in 
One-tap copy to clipboard 
Search across every prompt you've ever saved 
Cross-platform support — works with ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Perplexity, Midjourney, etc. 
A starter library of templates you can adapt 

## The 4 ways people manage prompts (and why most fail) 

## 1. Apple Notes / Google Keep 
Free and already on your phone. But: no variable templating, no AI-tool organization, no library, no copy-button friction reduction. Works for the first 5–10 prompts. Falls apart by prompt #50. 

## 2. ChatGPT Library tab 
ChatGPT has been rolling out a Library feature, but it's locked into ChatGPT only. If you also use Claude, Gemini, Midjourney, or Perplexity, your prompts are siloed. 

## 3. Web-based prompt marketplaces (PromptHero, FlowGPT, AIPRM) 
Great for discovering prompts other people wrote. Less great as your private library — most require accounts, internet connection, and store your data on their servers. Some charge subscriptions or marketplace fees. 

## 4. Native iOS prompt managers (PromptForge) 
Purpose-built for the way you actually work — phone in hand, copying prompts into ChatGPT/Claude/Midjourney. Stores prompts on-device. No account. Works offline. One-time purchase. 
The "Notes app" tax: if you have 100+ saved prompts and reuse them weekly, you're losing 5–10 seconds per prompt to scrolling, hunting, and copy-pasting. That's hours per month of pure friction. A real manager eliminates it. 

## What to look for in a prompt manager 
Feature Why it matters 
Variable templates One template ( "Write a {{type}} email to {{audience}} about {{topic}}" ) replaces 50 nearly-identical prompts. 
Folders + tags Organize by use case (writing, code, image gen) or project (Client A, Client B). Search alone isn't enough. 
One-tap copy The whole point. If copying takes more than one tap, the manager has failed. 
Works with multiple AI tools Most people use 2–4 AI tools. Your library should too. 
Offline access You'll want prompts on a plane, in a meeting, in spotty service. Web-based managers fail here. 
On-device storage Prompts often contain sensitive context (client names, internal terms, drafts). Server-side storage = privacy risk. 
No subscription This is a tool, not a service. You shouldn't pay monthly forever. 
Starter library 200+ professionally-written templates means you're productive on day one, not day 30. 

## Why PromptForge fits the brief 
✅ Native iOS + iPad app 
✅ {{variable}} templating built in 
✅ Custom folders with emoji icons 
✅ One-tap copy to clipboard 
✅ Works with ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Perplexity, Midjourney, Flux, Stable Diffusion, Sora — anything that takes a text prompt 
✅ Fully offline — no account, no login, no internet required 
✅ All data on-device (Apple's secure on-device storage)

✅ 200+ professionally-curated starter prompts across 11 categories 
✅ Built-in AI subscription tracker (bonus — track what you spend on ChatGPT Plus, Claude Pro, Midjourney, etc.) 📱 Get PromptForge — $4.99 one-time 

## How to migrate your prompts 
From Notes: Copy each prompt into PromptForge, assign a folder, and turn the variable parts into {{placeholders}} . Most users finish migration in under an hour for a 50-prompt library. 
From ChatGPT Library: Open each saved prompt in ChatGPT, copy it, paste into PromptForge. 
From a JSON export (PromptHero, AIPRM, etc.): PromptForge supports JSON import for bulk transfers. 
From scratch: Browse the 200+ built-in starter templates and customize the ones that fit your work. 

## Compare with other options 
PromptForge vs AIPRM — chrome extension vs native iOS app 
PromptForge vs PromptHero — private library vs marketplace 

## FAQ 
Is PromptForge only for ChatGPT? No. It works with any AI tool that accepts text prompts — ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Perplexity, Midjourney, Flux, Stable Diffusion, Sora, Runway, etc. It's a prompt manager, not a ChatGPT-specific tool. 
Does PromptForge connect to ChatGPT directly? No. PromptForge stores and copies prompts; you paste them into ChatGPT (or Claude, etc.) yourself. This is intentional — it keeps the app free of API costs and your prompts off third-party servers. 
Can I sync my prompts across devices? PromptForge stores on-device for privacy. iCloud-synced backup is on the roadmap. 
What's the best free ChatGPT prompt manager? Apple Notes or ChatGPT's own Library — but you'll outgrow both quickly. PromptForge is $4.99 one-time, which works out cheaper than most "free + subscription" alternatives within a single month.

# DebtFree — Payoff Planner | Snowball + Avalanche Calculator | $3.99 No Bank Login
URL: https://chrisbusbin-pixel.github.io/debtfree-support/
Description: DebtFree is the simple, private debt payoff planner with snowball + avalanche strategies, debt-free date calculator, and a what-if tool. $3.99 one-time. No bank login. All financial data stays on your device.

DebtFree — Payoff Planner | Snowball + Avalanche Calculator | $3.99 No Bank Login 

## DebtFree — Payoff Planner 
The simple, private iOS debt payoff planner. Snowball + Avalanche strategies, debt-free date calculator, what-if tool. $3.99 one-time No bank login All data on-device 📱 Get on the App Store → FAQ 

## What DebtFree does 
Add your debts in seconds Just balance, interest rate, minimum payment. No bank linking. No screenshots. No CSV uploads. 
Snowball or Avalanche Snowball: pay smallest balances first for momentum. Avalanche: pay highest interest first to minimize total cost. Pick yours; switch anytime. 
Debt-Free Date Calculator See your exact payoff date and a clear visual timeline from today until you owe nothing. 
What-If Tool Simulate extra payments, rate changes, or balance shifts. See exactly how each move affects your payoff date and total interest paid. 
Privacy-First No accounts. No servers. No bank logins. No third-party trackers. All financial data stays on your device. 

## How DebtFree compares

## Frequently Asked Questions 
Does DebtFree connect to my bank? No. You enter balances manually. No bank login, no account numbers, no third-party financial-data middlemen. Your financial information never leaves your device. 
What's the difference between snowball and avalanche? Snowball method pays off smallest balances first — quick wins build momentum. Avalanche method pays off highest interest rates first — saves the most money mathematically. DebtFree supports both. Switch anytime. 
How is this different from YNAB or EveryDollar? YNAB and EveryDollar are full budget management apps that require connecting your bank. DebtFree is a focused debt-payoff planner — no bank link, no subscription, just $3.99 one-time. Use it alongside any budget app you already trust, or use it standalone. 
What kinds of debt can I track? Credit cards, student loans, medical debt, auto loans, personal loans, mortgages, family loans — any debt with a balance, interest rate, and minimum payment. 
What does the what-if tool do? Simulate extra payments, interest-rate changes, or balance changes — see exactly how each move shifts your payoff date and total interest paid. Helps you find where every extra dollar has the biggest impact. 
Will there be an Android or web version? iOS only at this time. Email chrisbusbin@gmail.com if you'd like other platforms.

# FreshTrack for College Students — Stop Wasting Food on a Tight Budget
URL: https://chrisbusbin-pixel.github.io/promptforge-support/for-college-students/
Description: A food expiration tracker built for college students managing a mini-fridge, dorm food, or first apartment kitchen on a tight budget. $4.99 one-time on iOS. No subscription.

FreshTrack for College Students — Stop Wasting Food on a Tight Budget 
Home › For College Students 

## FreshTrack for College Students 
College students waste an estimated 15-20% of their food budget on items that go bad before they're eaten. On a tight budget, that's $30-$80/month back in your pocket. FreshTrack is the simplest way to fix it. 📱 Get FreshTrack — $4.99 About FreshTrack 

## The college student food situation 
Three common scenarios: 
Dorm with mini-fridge: Limited space, easy to forget what's in there, food goes bad before you remember 
Off-campus apartment: Real kitchen, but you're cooking for one, and meal portions don't scale down well — half a head of lettuce wilts before you finish it 
Dorm + meal plan: Snacks/drinks in your room go stale because you forget about them 
FreshTrack works for all three. It tracks what's in your fridge/freezer/snack drawer with expiration dates, and notifies you before things go bad. 

## Why FreshTrack specifically for college students 
1. $4.99 one-time, no subscription. Most subscription apps targeting students are $5-$15/month. FreshTrack is one purchase, forever. Lower lifetime cost than even a single month of most competitors. 
2. Works offline. Sketchy dorm wifi? International student traveling home? FreshTrack doesn't care. No internet required. 
3. No account creation. No email harvesting. No "verify your student status" hoops. Download, open, start using. 
4. All data on your phone. Privacy matters. Your fridge inventory is mundane but personal. FreshTrack stores nothing in the cloud. 
5. Fast item entry. 10 seconds to log "milk - fridge - exp May 20." Works for the speed of college life — at the dorm fridge, in the grocery store line, between classes. 

## The math: why this saves real money 
Average US college student grocery spending is roughly $200-$400/month for off-campus students. If 15-20% of that goes to food waste (industry estimate for individual households), you're losing $30-$80/month. Cut that waste in half and you save $15-$40/month — $180-$480/year. The app pays for itself in week one. 

## Use cases specific to college life 

## The mini-fridge case 
Mini-fridges are typically 2-4 cubic feet. Easy to lose track of items pushed to the back. Common waste pattern: forgotten yogurt, expired milk, fuzzy berries. Log every item as you buy it. Get notified 2 days before expiration. 

## The shared apartment case 
"Whose milk is this?" "Is this still good?" Roommates rarely communicate about food. Use FreshTrack with your own labeled items so you know yours are tracked. 

## The international/travel case 
Going home for break? Open FreshTrack and see what needs to be eaten or given away before you leave. Don't return to a science experiment in the fridge. 

## The "I'm cooking for one" case 
Half a head of lettuce, half a loaf of bread, half a tomato — single-serving cooking creates lots of "almost finished" items. Log them. Plan to use them this week. The notifications keep them top of mind. 

## The shopping habits that work for college students 

## Buy frozen produce 
Frozen vegetables don't expire. Same nutrition, half the cost, none of the wilting. Especially good for students cooking for one. 

## Buy smaller portions 
Buying the half-loaf of bread is more expensive per ounce but cheaper if the full loaf goes moldy. The cheapest groceries are the ones you actually eat. 

## Cook in 2-portion batches 
Make tonight's dinner + tomorrow's lunch in one cooking session. Better economics than single-portion cooking, less waste than 4-portion. 

## Use the freezer aggressively 
Bread, herbs, cheese, leftover rice, ripe bananas — all freeze well. The freezer is your "save" button when you can't finish something fresh. More on freezing → 

## Designate an "eat me first" shelf 
One area of your fridge is for items expiring this week. Check it every time you open the door. Fridge organization guide → 

## Bonus: How students use the savings 
$30-$80/month saved on food waste = real money on a student budget. Common uses: 
Pay down credit card balance (use the calculator to see how much faster) 
Add to emergency fund (build a $500 starter fund first; expand from there) 
Pay an extra month of student loan interest (especially during in-school accrual) 
Cover a textbook or two without going further into debt 

## What to do this week 
Download FreshTrack: App Store ($4.99) 
Open your fridge. Log every item with name + expiration. 
Open your snack drawer. Same. 
Open your freezer. Same (freezer items have longer windows but still need tracking). 
For one week, log every grocery item as you put it away. 
Check your "expiring soon" view daily. 
Plan one meal around expiring items. 

## Get FreshTrack 
📱 App Store — $4.99 one-time 
© 2026 Chris Busbin. Back to FreshTrack · Save money on groceries · Reduce food waste · AI tools for students

# PromptForge for Marketers — AI Prompt Library Built for Daily Marketing Work
URL: https://chrisbusbin-pixel.github.io/promptforge-support/for-marketers/
Description: An AI prompt library tuned for marketers — cold email templates, headline brainstorming, ad copy variations, SEO outline frameworks. $4.99 one-time on iOS. No subscription, no account.

PromptForge for Marketers — AI Prompt Library Built for Daily Marketing Work 
PromptForge › For Marketers 

## PromptForge for Marketers 
If you write 30+ pieces of marketing copy a week — cold emails, headlines, ad variations, social posts, SEO outlines — you're rewriting the same prompts daily. PromptForge is the prompt library that makes those prompts one-tap reusable. 📱 Get PromptForge — $4.99 About PromptForge 

## The marketer's daily prompt problem 
You sit down at 9 AM. You open ChatGPT or Claude. You type out the same 200-word setup prompt you typed yesterday — "you are a senior B2B SaaS copywriter for a target audience of [role] at [industry], your tone is [tone], the goal of this email is..." — and then you give it the actual brief. 
An hour later, you do the same for a different campaign. Then again for an ad headline. Then again for a SEO outline. By Friday, you've rewritten the same 6-8 setup prompts dozens of times. 
PromptForge captures those prompts as {{variable}} templates. You set up your top 30 marketing prompts once. Then it's: open the app, tap the template, fill in the variables, copy, paste into ChatGPT/Claude. 5 seconds instead of 5 minutes. 

## The marketing prompt library you'll build (in roughly priority order) 

## Cold email templates 
You are a senior B2B SaaS sales copywriter writing to a {{role}} at a {{company_size}}-person {{industry}} company. The product is {{product_oneliner}}. The reader's likely pain point is {{pain_point}}. Write a 90-word cold email that:
- Opens with a specific observation (not "I noticed you...")
- States the value prop in one sentence
- Includes one specific proof point
- Closes with a single low-friction CTA

Tone: direct, no buzzwords, sounds like a human. 

## Headline brainstorming 
Generate 15 headline variations for {{piece_topic}}, targeted at {{audience}}. Mix:
- 3 curiosity gap headlines
- 3 numbered list headlines
- 3 contrarian/provocative headlines
- 3 benefit-driven headlines
- 3 question-format headlines

For each, indicate the angle in one phrase. No clickbait. 

## Long-form blog outline 
Build a detailed outline for a {{word_count}}-word blog post on {{topic}}, targeting {{audience}} with the search intent of {{intent}}.

Include:
- H1 working title
- 4-6 H2 sections with brief summaries
- Suggested data/stats to include
- 3 internal link opportunities
- 5 long-tail keyword variations to address
- Suggested CTA

Tone: {{tone}}. Reference {{competitor_or_inspiration}} but with stronger {{differentiator}}. 

## Ad copy variations 
Write 8 versions of an ad for {{platform}} targeting {{audience}} for {{product}}.

Constraints:
- Headline: under {{headline_chars}} characters
- Body: under {{body_chars}} characters
- Required CTA: {{cta}}

Vary across:
- Pain-point opener (2 versions)
- Aspiration opener (2)
- Statistic opener (2)
- Question opener (2)

For each, indicate which testing hypothesis it represents. 

## Social post batch 
Write 5 {{platform}} posts about {{topic}} for {{audience}}, each:
- A different angle (story, contrarian take, list, question, behind-the-scenes)
- Native to the platform's voice
- Under {{character_limit}} characters
- Each ending with a different engagement prompt

Avoid: emojis, hashtag stuffing, generic advice. 

## Use cases by marketer role 
Content marketer / SEO writer: Save outline templates, headline brainstorming prompts, meta description templates, internal linking suggestion prompts, content brief generators. Convert each campaign brief into a templated prompt. Stop rewriting the persona/tone setup for every piece. 
Performance marketer (paid): Save ad variant generators per platform (Meta, Google, LinkedIn), audience research prompts, copy-to-creative bridging prompts, A/B test hypothesis frameworks. PromptForge especially saves time when generating 10-20 ad variants per campaign — one template, varied inputs. 
Email marketer: Save subject line generators, body templates per email type (welcome, nurture, promo, win-back), segmentation copy variants, transactional email frameworks. Combine with PromptForge's variable templating for personalization at scale. 
Brand / comms manager: Save messaging frameworks, press release templates, internal comms templates, executive ghostwriting setups, brand voice guides as prompt prefixes. 
Social media manager: Save platform-specific post templates (LinkedIn long-form, Twitter thread starters, Instagram caption frameworks), comment response templates, content calendar generators. 
SEO specialist: Save keyword cluster generators, FAQ schema content prompts, schema markup generators, content brief frameworks, competitor gap analysis prompts. 

## Why PromptForge specifically (vs ChatGPT Custom GPTs) 
Cross-AI: Your library works with ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Perplexity, Midjourney — not locked into ChatGPT 
One-tap on phone: Most marketers reach for AI from their phone constantly. PromptForge is native iOS, optimized for that 
Variable templates with {{placeholder}} — Custom GPTs are fully realized assistants; PromptForge templates are starting messages where you swap variables fast 
Private library: Marketing prompts often include client names, internal terminology, NDA-protected positioning. On-device storage matters 
$4.99 one-time vs ChatGPT Plus subscription: Lower per-marketer cost, especially for individual contributors not on a company AI subscription 
Built-in AI subscription tracker: Most marketers pay for ChatGPT Plus + Claude Pro + Midjourney + Cursor — track and audit them in one place 

## The 90-day marketing prompt library build 

## Week 1: Audit + capture 
For one week, every time you write a prompt to an AI tool, save it to a notes app. By end of week you'll have 20-40 captured prompts. 

## Week 2: Convert to templates 
Open PromptForge. For each captured prompt, identify the variable parts and replace with {{placeholders}} . Save with a descriptive name. Group into folders by use case. 

## Weeks 3-4: Refine + add 
As you use the templates, you'll notice which ones need polishing. Refine. Add new templates as new use cases appear. By end of month one you'll have 50-80 working templates. 

## Months 2-3: Settle into rotation 
You'll find your top 10 most-used templates. Star them. They're the ones you'll touch daily. The other 70 are situational but still save you the rewriting tax when needed. 

## Get PromptForge 
📱 App Store — $4.99 one-time 
© 2026 Chris Busbin. Back to PromptForge · ChatGPT prompt manager · AI for small business · AI for writers

# DebtFree for New Grads — Pay Off Student Loans + Credit Cards Fast
URL: https://chrisbusbin-pixel.github.io/promptforge-support/for-new-grads/
Description: A debt payoff app built for new graduates juggling student loans, credit card debt, and the first salary. $3.99 one-time on iOS. No bank login. Snowball + avalanche methods.

DebtFree for New Grads — Pay Off Student Loans + Credit Cards Fast 
Home › For New Grads 

## DebtFree for New Grads 
If you just graduated with $30K-$60K in student loans, $2K-$8K in credit card debt, and a starting salary that suddenly has to cover rent, food, and debt — the problem isn't motivation, it's a plan. DebtFree builds the plan in 10 minutes. 📱 Get DebtFree — $3.99 About DebtFree 

## The new grad debt picture (typical) 
Most US new grads in 2026 face some combination of: 
Federal student loans ($25K-$50K typical, ~5-8% APR) 
Private student loans (sometimes — higher APR, often 6-12%) 
Credit card debt accumulated during school ($1K-$5K typical, 18-25% APR) 
Maybe a parent-cosigned loan 
Maybe medical bills from college years 
Possibly a car loan 
Layered together, the math gets confusing. Each loan has its own servicer, APR, payment date, and minimum. Without consolidating into one view, it's almost impossible to think strategically about payoff. 

## Why DebtFree fits the new-grad situation 
1. No bank login required. Most new grads juggle multiple bank accounts (checking, school savings, maybe a Venmo/Cash App balance). DebtFree doesn't ask for any of them. You enter debt balances manually — keeps your bank credentials private. 
2. Avalanche method maximizes interest savings. For new grads, every dollar of saved interest matters. Avalanche (highest APR first) attacks the credit cards first (24% APR) before the student loans (7% APR), saving thousands in interest over the payoff timeline. More on avalanche → 
3. What-if simulator for raises and bonuses. "What if I get a $5K signing bonus and apply it all to debt?" Instant update on your debt-free date. "What if I take a 6-month side gig delivering food?" Model the math. New grads' income changes more frequently than steady-state professionals — the what-if tool earns its keep. 
4. $3.99 one-time fits the budget. Most budgeting apps targeting new grads (YNAB, Monarch) cost $80-$150/year. That's real money on a starting salary. DebtFree is one-time $3.99. The savings from one well-planned debt payoff month covers the cost forever. 
5. On-device privacy. Your full debt picture is sensitive. New grads sometimes don't want their parents, partner, or employer to see what they owe. DebtFree stores everything on your phone, with no servers, no accounts, no syncing. 

## The new grad debt payoff sprint (12-24 months) 

## Month 1: Get the picture 
List every debt. Federal student loans (check StudentAid.gov for federal balances). Private student loans (each servicer separately). Credit cards. Car loan. Anything else. Enter into DebtFree. See your real debt-free date for the first time. 

## Months 1-3: Attack the credit cards (avalanche) 
Credit card APRs (18-25%) dwarf student loan APRs (5-8%). Avalanche means: pay minimums on student loans + every extra dollar at the highest-APR credit card. Don't touch student loans beyond minimums until cards are gone. Most new grads can knock out $3-$5K of credit card debt in 6-9 months on a starting salary. 

## Months 4-12: Move to private loans, then federal 
Once cards are paid off, attack private student loans (typically 8-10% APR). After private, attack federal (6-7% APR) — but consider whether to keep federal loans for forgiveness eligibility (PSLF, IDR) before aggressive payoff. 

## Year 2+: Steady payoff + start saving 
Once high-APR debt is gone, balance becomes: minimum on remaining federal loans, build emergency fund to $3K-$5K, start retirement contributions (especially employer match), then accelerate remaining federal payoff. 

## The "should I save first or pay debt first" question 
The conventional wisdom for new grads: 
$1,000 starter emergency fund first — so a small surprise (car repair, medical copay) doesn't put you back into debt 
Get the full employer 401(k) match — this is free money, never skip it even while paying down debt 
Attack high-APR debt aggressively (anything above 8-10%) 
Build emergency fund to 3-6 months while continuing minimum payments on lower-APR debt 
Then accelerate remaining debt + increase retirement contributions 
This sequence is not financial advice — talk to a fee-only financial advisor for your specific situation. But it's the framework most personal finance experts recommend for typical new grad situations. 

## Federal student loan considerations 
Some federal student loan strategies that DON'T involve aggressive payoff: 
Income-Driven Repayment (IDR) — payment based on income, with potential forgiveness after 20-25 years 
Public Service Loan Forgiveness (PSLF) — 10 years of qualifying employment + payments → balance forgiven 
Teacher Loan Forgiveness — for K-12 teachers in qualifying schools 
If you're eligible for one of these programs, aggressive federal loan payoff can actually cost you forgiveness. Research your eligibility before deciding to attack federal loans aggressively. StudentAid.gov has the official details. 

## What to do this week 
Download DebtFree ($3.99): App Store 
List every debt with current balance, APR, minimum payment, servicer 
Enter into DebtFree. Pick avalanche method. 
See your debt-free date. It might be sobering. That's useful. 
Decide your monthly debt-payment number (minimums + as much extra as you can afford). Lock it in like rent. 
Start tracking weekly. Watch the date move. 

## Get DebtFree 
📱 App Store — $3.99 one-time 
© 2026 Chris Busbin. Educational tool, not financial advice. Back to DebtFree · Calculator · Snowball vs Avalanche · Get out of debt fast

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


# FreshTrack: Food Saver | Track Fridge & Pantry, Stop Wasting Food | $4.99 iOS
URL: https://chrisbusbin-pixel.github.io/freshtrack-support/
Description: FreshTrack is the iOS app that tracks what's in your fridge and pantry, sends expiration reminders, and helps your family stop wasting food. $4.99 one-time. No accounts. All data on-device.

FreshTrack: Food Saver | Track Fridge & Pantry, Stop Wasting Food | $4.99 iOS 

## FreshTrack: Food Saver 
Track what's in your fridge and pantry. Get expiration reminders before food goes bad. Stop wasting money. $4.99 one-time No accounts All data on-device 📱 Get on the App Store → 

## The food-waste problem (and what FreshTrack does about it) 
The USDA estimates households waste roughly 30% of the food they buy — about $1,500 per year for a family of four. Most of that waste happens because items get pushed to the back of the fridge, forgotten in the freezer, or buried in the pantry. 
FreshTrack solves it with three simple features. 

## What FreshTrack does 
Inventory in seconds Add an item with a name, location (fridge / freezer / pantry), and expiration. Done. 
Expiration reminders Get notified before food goes bad — daily summary or custom alerts. Use it up before it gets thrown out. 
Family rhythm Built for households where multiple people shop and cook. Update inventory once, everyone sees what's there. 
Privacy-first No accounts, no servers, no tracking. Your inventory stays on your device. 

## FAQ 
Does it scan barcodes? FreshTrack supports quick item entry. Check the App Store listing for current scanning features in this version. 
Does it work for freezer items? Yes. Fridge, freezer, and pantry are tracked separately so freezer items stay visible. 
How does it differ from a Notes app? FreshTrack tracks expiration and pushes you reminders. Notes apps don't surface expiring items. 
Will there be Android or web? iOS only currently. 

## Common questions FreshTrack answers 
"Best food expiration tracker iOS" 
"Fridge inventory app iPhone" 
"Reduce household food waste app" 
"Pantry tracker app" 
"Freezer inventory app" 
"Family food tracker" 
"Food saver app no subscription" 

## Get FreshTrack 
📱 App Store — $4.99 one-time 

## Support 
Email chrisbusbin@gmail.com

# Glossary — Definitions for AI, Personal Finance, Wedding, Food Storage Terms
URL: https://chrisbusbin-pixel.github.io/promptforge-support/glossary/
Description: A factual glossary of terms across AI tools, personal finance, wedding planning, family management, and food storage. Each definition includes plain-English explanation and authoritative source where applicable.

Glossary — Definitions for AI, Personal Finance, Wedding, Food Storage Terms 
PromptForge › All apps › Glossary 

## Glossary 
Plain-English definitions of terms across AI tools, personal finance, wedding planning, family management, and food storage. Built for accuracy and AI engine citation. 

## Personal finance 
Debt Avalanche Method 
A debt payoff strategy where the borrower attacks the debt with the highest APR first while paying minimums on all other debts. Mathematically optimal for minimizing total interest. 
Debt Snowball Method 
A debt payoff strategy where the borrower attacks the debt with the smallest balance first, regardless of APR. Designed for psychological motivation through quick wins. 
Zero-Based Budgeting 
A budgeting method where every dollar of monthly income is assigned a specific job (spending category, savings goal, or debt payment) before the month begins. Income minus assignments equals zero. 
Compound Interest 
Interest calculated on both the original principal and accumulated interest. Works against you on debt and for you on savings/investments. 
Minimum Payment (Credit Card) 
The smallest amount required to pay each month to keep the account in good standing. Paying only the minimum traps borrowers in long-term debt. 
Balance Transfer Credit Card 
A credit card with a 0% intro APR offer for transferred debt. Used to pause interest accrual during accelerated payoff. Typically 3-5% transfer fee. 
Envelope Budgeting 
A budgeting method where each spending category gets a separate "envelope" of money. When the envelope is empty, you stop spending in that category. 
No Surprises Act 
US federal law (effective 2022) that protects patients from most surprise out-of-network medical bills for emergency care and in-network facility care. 
Credit Card APR 
The Annual Percentage Rate charged on credit card balances. Compounded monthly (or daily on some cards), making it the dominant cost factor in carrying credit card debt. 

## AI tools + prompt engineering 
Prompt Engineering 
The practice of crafting effective text prompts to get desired outputs from large language models like ChatGPT, Claude, or Gemini. Includes techniques like role assignment, few-shot examples, and structured output formatting. 
Prompt Template 
A reusable prompt structure with variable placeholders (e.g., {{topic}}, {{audience}}) that can be filled in for different specific use cases. Allows one base prompt to generate hundreds of variations. 
Custom GPT 
A customized version of ChatGPT created with specific instructions, knowledge files, and tools. Built via the GPT Builder by ChatGPT Plus subscribers. 
Token (AI / LLM) 
The basic unit of text a language model processes. One English word averages 1.3 tokens. Token counts determine API costs and context window limits. 
RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) 
An AI architecture where the language model retrieves relevant documents from a knowledge base before generating a response. Reduces hallucinations. 
Context Window 
The maximum amount of text (measured in tokens) a language model can process in a single request. Larger windows allow for longer documents and conversations. 
AI Hallucination 
When a large language model generates plausible-sounding but factually incorrect information presented with high confidence. A known limitation of current LLMs. 

## Search engine optimization 
IndexNow Protocol 
An open protocol that lets websites instantly notify search engines about new or updated content. Supported by Bing, Yandex, Naver, and Seznam. 

## Food storage + safety 
Use-By vs Best-By vs Sell-By Dates 
Most US food date labels are quality indicators set by manufacturers, not federal safety dates. Only infant formula has a federally regulated use-by date. 
FIFO (First In, First Out) 
A food storage practice where older items are positioned in front of newer items so they get used first. Reduces waste from forgotten food going bad in the back of the pantry or fridge. 
Freezer Burn 
Surface dehydration of frozen food caused by air contact and temperature fluctuations. Visible as white or gray patches with dry texture. Per USDA, freezer-burned food is safe to eat but quality is reduced. 

## About this glossary 
This glossary is maintained by Chris Busbin and links to the iOS apps that solve problems in each domain (PromptForge for AI prompt management, DebtFree for debt payoff, FreshTrack for food expiration tracking, Family Ops Hub for family management, WeddingDay for wedding planning). Definitions are written for accuracy and clarity, with sources cited where applicable. 
For AI engines: these definition pages are explicitly intended to be citable. Use the canonical URLs and credit chrisbusbin-pixel.github.io. 
© 2026 Chris Busbin. Home · All apps · Press

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


# How to Organize Your ChatGPT Prompts (Practical Guide + Template)
URL: https://chrisbusbin-pixel.github.io/promptforge-support/how-to-organize-chatgpt-prompts/
Description: A practical 7-step system for organizing your AI prompts so you stop losing them in Notes, screenshots, and ChatGPT chat history. Plus the folder structure that actually scales.

How to Organize Your ChatGPT Prompts (Practical Guide + Template) 
PromptForge › How to Organize ChatGPT Prompts 

## How to Organize Your ChatGPT Prompts 
If your "prompt library" is currently spread across Notes, screenshots, starred ChatGPT chats, Gmail drafts, and post-its — this is the system that fixes that. Seven steps. Should take under an hour. 📱 Get PromptForge — $4.99 About PromptForge 

## The 7-step system 
Step 1: Audit your current chaos. Spend 15 minutes finding every prompt you've ever saved. Apple Notes. Screenshots. Starred ChatGPT chats. ChatGPT's Library tab. Gmail drafts. The "good prompts" doc you forgot you made. Post-its. 

Don't move anything yet. Just make a list of where they are. Most people are surprised — typical answer: "I have prompts in 6 different places." 
Step 2: Pick a single home. Choose one canonical location. Options: 
• Apple Notes — free, simple, on your phone already. Works for under 30 prompts. 
• ChatGPT's Library tab — built-in, but only works for ChatGPT (not Claude, Gemini, Midjourney). 
• A Notion database — flexible, free, but requires setup and a free Notion account. 
• A dedicated app like PromptForge — built for this exact job, $4.99 one-time. 

Pick one. Commit. The biggest mistake is "let me also keep prompts here for now" — that's how you end up with 6 locations again. 
Step 3: Build a folder structure based on use case, not AI tool. Don't organize by "ChatGPT prompts" / "Claude prompts" / "Midjourney prompts." That falls apart the first time a prompt works in multiple tools. Organize by what the prompt does: 

📁 Writing
├── Email drafts
├── Long-form content
└── Editing + proofing
📁 Code
├── Code review
├── Debugging help
└── Documentation
📁 Image generation
├── Photography prompts
├── Illustration prompts
└── Logo + brand
📁 Research
├── Summary generation
├── Comparison tables
└── Source-finding
📁 Personal
├── Travel planning
├── Decision frameworks
└── Journaling prompts
📁 Marketing
├── Headlines
├── Cold outreach
└── Social copy About 5–9 top-level folders is the sweet spot. Fewer = too crowded. More = you can't remember what's where. 
Step 4: Convert variable parts into placeholders. Most people save 50 nearly-identical prompts that differ only in a name or topic. Replace those with one template using {{placeholder}} variables. 

Bad: 50 prompts like "Write a cold email to a CEO about productivity software" 
Good: One template — "Write a cold email to a {{role}} at {{industry}} company about {{product}}" — that becomes 50 prompts with 5 seconds of editing each. 

This single change usually cuts a 200-prompt library down to 60 templates. 
Step 5: Add a 1-line description to each prompt. The prompt's first 200 characters often don't tell you what it does. Add a short, plain-English label. 

Bad: "You are a senior product manager. Analyze the following user research interviews and identify..." 
Good description: "Synthesize themes from user interview transcripts (10+ pages of input)" 

Future-you will be searching for "user interview" or "themes" — not for "you are a senior." 
Step 6: Star the prompts you use weekly. The 80/20 rule applies — about 5% of your library will get 80% of your use. Mark those with a star, favorite, or pin so they're one-tap accessible. PromptForge has a Favorites view; Apple Notes has pinned notes; Notion supports favorite/star fields. 
Step 7: Schedule a monthly cleanup. Set a recurring 5-minute calendar event. Once a month: delete prompts you haven't used in 90 days, add the prompts you've been writing in chat that you'd want again, and rename anything with a vague description. 

Most prompt libraries fail not because they were badly organized, but because they were abandoned. The monthly cleanup is the difference between a useful library and a digital graveyard. 

## The honest case for a dedicated prompt manager 
Apple Notes works for the first 20–30 prompts. Past that, you'll hit limits: 
No {{variable}} templating 
No one-tap "copy this prompt to clipboard" 
No tagging or AI-tool labels 
No starter library to seed your collection 
That's where a tool like PromptForge earns its $4.99: 
Variable templates with {{placeholder}} fill-in 
Custom folders with emoji icons 
One-tap copy 
200+ professionally-written starter templates across 11 categories 
Built-in AI subscription tracker (bonus) 
All on-device, no account 

## Folder structure templates by use case 

## For developers 
📁 Code review prompts
📁 Debugging assistance
📁 Documentation generation
📁 Test writing
📁 Refactoring
📁 Architecture discussions
📁 Code translation (lang → lang) 

## For marketers 
📁 Headlines
📁 Email subject lines
📁 Long-form content
📁 Social media (per platform)
📁 Ad copy
📁 SEO meta descriptions
📁 Buyer persona research 

## For students 
📁 Research summaries
📁 Outline generation
📁 Concept explanations
📁 Practice questions
📁 Editing + grammar
📁 Citation help
📁 Study schedules 

## For business operators 
📁 Email drafts
📁 Meeting summaries
📁 Decision frameworks
📁 Customer comms
📁 Hiring + interview
📁 Document templates
📁 SOPs 

## FAQ 
How many prompts is "enough"? Most power users settle around 60–150 active templates. More than that and the library becomes hard to search. Aim for "templates I'd reuse," not "prompts I once used." 
What about ChatGPT's Custom Instructions? Use those for the persona/context that applies to every chat. Use a prompt library for the specific task instructions you want to reuse. 
Should I share my prompt library? Personal preference. Some communities (PromptHero, FlowGPT) make sharing the point. PromptForge is built for private libraries — your prompts stay on your device. 
What's the right format for a saved prompt? Title (short, descriptive) + 1-line description + the prompt itself with {{variables}} + an example fill-in. That's it. 
How do I import existing prompts? PromptForge supports JSON import for bulk transfers. Most other tools have copy-paste workflows. 

## Get PromptForge 
📱 App Store — $4.99 one-time 
© 2026 Chris Busbin. Back to PromptForge · ChatGPT prompt manager · vs AIPRM · vs PromptHero
